quiz.question: What does box-sizing: border-box do?
quiz.explanation: border-box makes declared width include padding and border. The default content-box does not.

quiz.question: What is the order of box model layers from inside out?
quiz.explanation: Content → padding → border → margin. Padding is inside the border; margin is outside.

quiz.question: What happens when two vertical margins meet between block elements?
quiz.explanation: Vertical margin collapse: adjacent top/bottom margins collapse to the larger value.

quiz.question: What is the default value of box-sizing?
quiz.explanation: The default is content-box, where width applies only to the content area — padding and border add extra size.

quiz.question: What does overflow: hidden create besides hiding overflow?
quiz.explanation: overflow: hidden creates a BFC which clears internal floats and prevents margin collapse.

quiz.question: What does display: inline-block allow?
options: Inline flow with block-level sizing (width/height work)
quiz.explanation: inline-block elements flow with text but accept width, height, and vertical padding like block elements.

quiz.question: When does margin collapse NOT occur?
options: Between flex/grid children or elements with non-visible overflow
quiz.explanation: Flex and grid containers create formatting contexts preventing margin collapse. overflow: hidden/auto also prevents it.

quiz.question: What is the visual formatting model?
options: How browsers position block, inline, and formatting contexts
quiz.explanation: The visual formatting model defines how block/inline/flex/grid formatting contexts place boxes on screen.

quiz.question: What does outline differ from border in the box model?
options: Outline does not affect layout dimensions
quiz.explanation: Outline is drawn outside the border but does not affect layout — it adds no space.

quiz.question: What does the aspect-ratio CSS property do?
options: Maintains a width-to-height ratio as element resizes
quiz.explanation: aspect-ratio: 16/9 reserves proportional space before content loads, preventing CLS.

quiz.question: What is the default value of the CSS position property?
quiz.explanation: position: static is the default. Static elements follow normal document flow.

quiz.question: What does position: absolute remove the element from?
quiz.explanation: Absolute elements are taken out of normal flow — siblings act as if they do not exist.

quiz.question: Which positioning value is relative to the nearest positioned ancestor?
quiz.explanation: Absolute elements are positioned relative to the nearest ancestor with position other than static.

quiz.question: What is the difference between fixed and sticky?
options: fixed is always viewport-relative; sticky toggles between relative and fixed based on scroll
quiz.explanation: Fixed stays relative to viewport always. Sticky acts like relative until a scroll threshold, then sticks.

quiz.question: What does z-index control?
options: Stacking order along the Z axis
quiz.explanation: z-index sets stacking order within a stacking context. Only works on positioned elements.

quiz.question: What creates a new stacking context?
quiz.explanation: Many properties create stacking contexts: position+z-index, opacity<1, transform, filter, will-change, isolation, etc.

quiz.question: What is the containing block for position: fixed?
options: Usually the viewport unless transform/filter ancestor exists
quiz.explanation: Fixed uses the viewport as containing block — unless an ancestor has transform, filter, or perspective.

quiz.question: When does a sticky element stop sticking?
options: When it reaches the viewport edge
When its parent container scrolls out of view
quiz.explanation: Sticky elements are constrained to their parent's bounding box — they stop when the parent scrolls away.

quiz.question: What does isolation: isolate do?
options: Creates a new stacking context without visual side effects
quiz.explanation: isolation: isolate creates a new stacking context — useful without needing opacity/transform side effects.

quiz.question: What is the inset shorthand?
quiz.explanation: inset: 0 equals top:0; right:0; bottom:0; left:0 — great for filling a containing block absolutely.

quiz.question: What CSS property enables Flexbox?
quiz.explanation: display: flex on a container makes its direct children flex items.

quiz.question: What is the default value of flex-direction?
quiz.explanation: flex-direction: row arranges flex items horizontally by default.

quiz.question: What does justify-content control?
quiz.explanation: justify-content aligns items along the main axis. align-items controls the cross axis.

quiz.question: What does flex: 1 expand to?
quiz.explanation: flex: 1 = flex-grow:1; flex-shrink:1; flex-basis:0%. Items grow to fill space equally.

quiz.question: What does align-self do?
options: Overrides align-items for a single item
quiz.explanation: align-self on a flex item overrides the container's align-items for that specific item.

quiz.question: What does flex-wrap: wrap do?
options: Allows items to wrap onto multiple lines
quiz.explanation: By default items shrink or overflow. flex-wrap: wrap lets items break onto new lines.

quiz.question: What is the difference between flex-basis and width?
options: flex-basis sets initial size before grow/shrink; width applies after
quiz.explanation: flex-basis is the starting size before flex-grow/shrink adjustments. In a flex container it overrides width on the main axis.

quiz.question: What does align-content do?
options: Aligns multiple flex lines when there is extra cross-axis space
quiz.explanation: align-content controls spacing of wrapped lines. Has no effect with only one line.

quiz.question: How do you create a sticky footer with Flexbox?
quiz.explanation: Set min-height:100vh + flex-direction:column on wrapper, then flex:1 on main content so it grows to push footer down.

quiz.question: What does the order property change?
options: Visual render order without changing DOM order
quiz.explanation: order changes visual position within flex/grid. Default is 0; lower values appear first. Does not affect tab order.

quiz.question: Which property defines column tracks in CSS Grid?
quiz.explanation: grid-template-columns defines the number and size of columns, e.g., 1fr 2fr 1fr.

quiz.question: What does the fr unit represent?
quiz.explanation: 1fr = one fraction of remaining space after fixed tracks are allocated.

quiz.question: What does repeat(3, 1fr) equal?
quiz.explanation: repeat(3, 1fr) is shorthand for 1fr 1fr 1fr — three equal columns.

quiz.question: How do you make a grid item span 2 columns?
quiz.explanation: grid-column: span 2 makes the item occupy 2 column tracks.

quiz.question: What is the difference between grid-template-areas and grid-area?
options: grid-template-areas defines the layout map on container; grid-area names an item
quiz.explanation: grid-template-areas on the container defines a visual ASCII map. grid-area on an item assigns it a named area.

quiz.question: What does minmax(200px, 1fr) do?
options: Creates a track at least 200px that grows to fill available space
quiz.explanation: minmax() sets minimum and maximum track sizes. minmax(200px, 1fr) means at least 200px but as large as available.

quiz.question: What is the difference between auto-fill and auto-fit in repeat()?
options: auto-fill keeps empty tracks; auto-fit collapses empty tracks
auto-fill works only with fixed widths
quiz.explanation: Both repeat as many tracks as fit. auto-fill keeps empty tracks; auto-fit collapses them letting non-empty tracks grow.

quiz.question: What does place-items: center do on a grid container?
options: Centers all grid items both horizontally and vertically
quiz.explanation: place-items: center is shorthand for align-items: center and justify-items: center.

quiz.question: What is a grid implicit grid?
options: Tracks auto-created when items exceed the explicit grid definition
quiz.explanation: When items overflow the explicit grid, the browser creates implicit tracks sized by grid-auto-rows/columns.

quiz.question: What does grid-auto-flow: dense do?
options: Allows the algorithm to backfill holes with smaller items
quiz.explanation: dense packing attempts to fill gaps left by larger items. Without it, items follow DOM order and gaps remain.

quiz.question: What CSS rule enables responsive styles at specific widths?
quiz.explanation: @media queries apply CSS only when specified conditions like max-width are met.

quiz.question: What does the viewport meta tag do?
options: Tells mobile browsers to use device width for layout
quiz.explanation: width=device-width, initial-scale=1 is essential — without it mobile browsers zoom out to simulate desktop.

quiz.question: What is mobile-first design?
options: Writing base styles for mobile then adding larger-screen styles with min-width queries
quiz.explanation: Mobile-first: start with mobile CSS, add complexity with min-width breakpoints. Opposite of desktop-first (max-width).

quiz.question: What does the clamp() function do?
options: Sets min, preferred, and max value: clamp(min, ideal, max)
quiz.explanation: clamp(1rem, 2.5vw, 2rem) uses 2.5vw but never below 1rem or above 2rem — fluid responsive sizing.

quiz.question: What CSS unit is relative to viewport width?
quiz.explanation: vw = 1% of viewport width. 100vw = full width. vh = viewport height.

quiz.question: What does @media (prefers-color-scheme: dark) do?
options: Applies dark styles to all users
Applies styles only when the OS is in dark mode
quiz.explanation: prefers-color-scheme detects OS dark/light preference and applies appropriate styles automatically.

quiz.question: What is a container query?
options: @container applies styles based on the parent container size, not the viewport
quiz.explanation: @container lets components respond to their containing element size rather than the global viewport.

quiz.question: What is the difference between min-width and max-width in media queries?
options: min-width is mobile-first (progressive enhancement); max-width is desktop-first
quiz.explanation: min-width starts small and adds. max-width starts large and removes. Mobile-first (min-width) is generally recommended.

quiz.question: What does the aspect-ratio property do?
options: Maintains a width-to-height ratio as element resizes
quiz.explanation: aspect-ratio: 16/9 ensures proportional sizing regardless of width, eliminating JavaScript ratio hacks.

quiz.question: What is fluid typography?
options: Text size that scales fluidly between min and max using clamp() and viewport units
quiz.explanation: font-size: clamp(1rem, 1rem + 1vw, 1.5rem) scales smoothly between breakpoints without discrete jumps.

quiz.question: Which property animates a triggered state change?
quiz.explanation: transition defines how a property change animates over time when triggered, e.g., on :hover.

quiz.question: What does transform: translateX(50px) do?
options: Moves element 50px right without affecting layout
quiz.explanation: translateX moves the element visually without affecting document flow or other elements.

quiz.question: What does animation-fill-mode: forwards do?
options: Retains final keyframe styles after animation ends
quiz.explanation: forwards keeps the element at the last keyframe state. Without it the element snaps back.

quiz.question: Which CSS properties are safest to animate for performance?
quiz.explanation: opacity and transform are composited by the GPU and do not trigger layout or paint.

quiz.question: What does animation-timing-function: cubic-bezier() allow?
options: Custom easing curves beyond preset keywords
quiz.explanation: cubic-bezier(x1,y1,x2,y2) defines a custom acceleration curve enabling bounce and elastic effects.

quiz.question: What does transform-origin control?
options: The point around which transforms are applied (default: center center)
quiz.explanation: transform-origin sets the pivot point for rotate/scale. top left rotates from the top-left corner.

quiz.question: What is the difference between transition and animation?
options: transition reacts to state changes; animation runs independently via @keyframes
quiz.explanation: Transitions require a trigger. Animations run autonomously via @keyframes and can loop, delay, and alternate.

quiz.question: What does @media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce) affect?
options: Users preferring less motion — animations should be minimal or removed
quiz.explanation: Respecting prefers-reduced-motion is a WCAG requirement for users with vestibular disorders.

quiz.question: How does will-change help CSS animations?
options: Hints browser to promote element to GPU layer before animation starts
quiz.explanation: will-change: transform tells the browser to composite the element ahead of time, preventing jank at animation start.

quiz.question: What is a CSS compositing layer?
options: A GPU texture for elements that can be animated without affecting the rest of the page
quiz.explanation: Composited layers are GPU textures. The compositor can move/fade them without re-painting other content.

quiz.question: What does BEM stand for?
quiz.explanation: BEM = Block__Element--Modifier. A naming convention for scalable CSS.

quiz.question: What is the purpose of CSS custom properties?
options: Store reusable values centrally that cascade everywhere
quiz.explanation: CSS custom properties (--primary: #3b82f6) enable theming, reduce repetition, and can be updated via JS.

quiz.question: What is the utility-first CSS approach?
options: Compose designs using small single-purpose utility classes in HTML
quiz.explanation: Utility-first keeps styles co-located with markup, eliminates naming decisions, and reduces CSS bundle growth.

quiz.question: What problem does CSS Modules solve?
options: Global CSS scope — class names are locally scoped to the component file
quiz.explanation: CSS Modules transform class names to unique hashes at build time, eliminating global class name collisions.

quiz.question: What is CSS-in-JS?
options: Writing CSS inside JavaScript files with component scoping and dynamic values
quiz.explanation: CSS-in-JS libraries (styled-components, Emotion) generate scoped class names and allow dynamic styles.

quiz.question: What is a design token?
options: A named design decision (color, spacing, typography) stored as a variable shareable across platforms
quiz.explanation: Design tokens (--color-primary, --spacing-4) abstract visual decisions enabling consistent cross-platform theming.

quiz.question: What is the SMACSS approach?
options: Scalable and Modular Architecture for CSS — categorizes rules into Base, Layout, Module, State, Theme
quiz.explanation: SMACSS organizes CSS into purpose layers: Base (resets), Layout (structure), Module (components), State, Theme.

quiz.question: What is critical CSS?
options: The minimum CSS needed to render above-the-fold content — inlined in head to eliminate render blocking
CSS that must not be changed
quiz.explanation: Inlining critical CSS eliminates a render-blocking request. Non-critical CSS loads asynchronously.

quiz.question: What does CSS containment (contain property) do?
options: Isolates subtrees from the rest of the page for performance optimization
quiz.explanation: contain: layout tells the browser layout changes inside cannot affect outside — enables subtree optimization.

quiz.question: What is the Single Responsibility Principle in CSS?
options: Each class should do one thing only
quiz.explanation: SRP in CSS means .center-text only centers text, .bg-primary only sets background — compose small classes.

quiz.question: What triggers a browser repaint?
options: Changing visual properties like color or background without layout change
quiz.explanation: Repaints happen when visual styles change but layout is unaffected — cheaper than reflows but still costly at scale.

quiz.question: What triggers a reflow?
options: Changing geometric properties: width, height, margin, padding
quiz.explanation: Reflows recalculate the geometry of all affected elements — the most expensive rendering operation.

quiz.question: Which CSS property change is handled by compositor only (no layout or paint)?
quiz.explanation: opacity and transform changes are handled by GPU compositor without triggering layout or paint.

quiz.question: What is jank in web performance?
options: Visible frame drops below 60fps causing stuttering
quiz.explanation: Jank occurs when frames take longer than 16ms, causing visible stuttering from heavy main thread work.

quiz.question: What does requestAnimationFrame do?
options: Schedules a callback before the next browser paint
quiz.explanation: rAF fires callbacks just before the browser renders the next frame, enabling smooth 60fps JS animations.

quiz.question: What does content-visibility: auto do?
options: Skips rendering of off-screen content until it approaches the viewport
quiz.explanation: content-visibility: auto skips layout and paint for off-screen elements — can cut render time 50%+ on long pages.

quiz.question: What is layout thrashing?
options: Repeatedly interleaving DOM reads and writes forcing repeated synchronous reflows
quiz.explanation: Reading offsetWidth forces a flush. Writing style invalidates. Reading again forces another flush. Batch reads then writes.

quiz.question: What does CSS contain: layout tell the browser?
options: Changes inside this subtree cannot affect outside — browser can skip layout for the rest
quiz.explanation: CSS containment enables the browser to skip layout recalculation for the rest of the page when this subtree changes.

quiz.question: What is the compositor thread?
options: A browser thread that handles scroll and transform animations without blocking the main thread
quiz.explanation: The compositor runs separately from JS. Scroll and transform animations on compositor layers run at 60fps even when JS is busy.

quiz.question: What does will-change: transform optimize?
options: Promotes the element to its own GPU layer to avoid repaints during animation
quiz.explanation: will-change promotes the element to a separate GPU layer so animating it does not trigger repaints of other elements.

coding.title: Box Sizing Comparison
coding.problemStatement: Create two divs both with width:200px, padding:20px, border:2px. One uses content-box (default), the other uses border-box. Calculate and show the actual rendered width of each.
coding.examples: content-box: 200+40+4=244px. border-box: exactly 200px
coding.hints: border-box includes padding and border in declared width. content-box adds them on top.
coding.solutionExplanation: border-box is predictable: declared width IS the rendered width. This is why global box-sizing: border-box is recommended.

coding.title: Margin Collapse Detector
coding.problemStatement: Write a JS function that detects if two vertically adjacent elements are experiencing margin collapse by comparing the expected gap (sum of margins) to the actual measured gap.
coding.examples: el1.marginBottom=20, el2.marginTop=20 but actual gap=20 (collapsed, not 40)
coding.hints: getBoundingClientRect gives actual pixel positions. Compare expected vs actual gap.
coding.solutionExplanation: When the actual gap equals the larger margin (not the sum), collapse has occurred.

coding.title: Responsive Card Grid
coding.problemStatement: Build a card grid using CSS Grid with box-sizing: border-box. Cards should be exactly 300px wide with 24px padding, 1px border, and a 16px gap between them. Make it 3 columns on desktop, 2 on tablet, 1 on mobile.
coding.examples: grid-template-columns: repeat(3, 300px) with gap:16px
coding.hints: border-box makes the 300px width include padding and border. Use 1fr for fluid columns on mobile.
coding.solutionExplanation: With border-box, all cards are exactly 300px wide regardless of padding. The gap property adds space between grid items.

coding.title: Element Size Calculator
coding.problemStatement: Write a function that returns the complete size information for a DOM element: content width/height, padding, border, margin, offset dimensions, client dimensions, and scroll dimensions.
coding.examples: {contentW, contentH, paddingTop, borderTop, marginTop, offsetWidth, clientWidth, scrollWidth}
coding.hints: offsetWidth includes padding+border. clientWidth includes padding but not border. scrollWidth includes overflow content.
coding.solutionExplanation: The three width types: offsetWidth=content+padding+border, clientWidth=content+padding, scrollWidth=full scrollable content.

coding.title: Center Everything
coding.problemStatement: Write CSS to center a 400x300 modal perfectly in the viewport using 3 different techniques: (1) flexbox on body, (2) absolute positioning with transform, (3) CSS Grid with place-items.
coding.examples: Three working centering techniques
coding.hints: Flexbox needs justify-content + align-items. Transform -50% offsets from center. place-items is shorthand.
coding.solutionExplanation: All three work but grid with place-items is the most concise. The transform trick works for any unknown size element.

coding.title: Overflow Scroll Container
coding.problemStatement: Build a scrollable sidebar with custom thin scrollbar styling. The sidebar should be fixed height 400px, overflow-y:auto, and show a custom blue scrollbar with rounded thumb.
coding.examples: Custom scrollbar for Webkit + scrollbar-width:thin for Firefox
coding.hints: Two APIs needed: ::-webkit-scrollbar for Chrome/Safari, scrollbar-width for Firefox.
coding.solutionExplanation: scrollbar-width: thin + scrollbar-color is the modern standard (Firefox). ::-webkit-scrollbar is the WebKit-specific API.

coding.title: Full Bleed Layout
coding.problemStatement: Create a blog post layout where the main content is max-width:680px centered, but certain elements (images, code blocks) extend to full viewport width. Use negative margins to break out of the container.
coding.examples: margin-left: calc(-50vw + 50%) pulls element to viewport edge
coding.hints: calc(-50vw + 50%) = half viewport minus half container. This extends element to viewport edge.
coding.solutionExplanation: The full-bleed calc: 50vw is half viewport, 50% is half of the current container. Their difference breaks out to the viewport edge.

coding.title: Box Shadow Elevation System
coding.problemStatement: Build a 5-level elevation system using box-shadow. Each level should be more pronounced. Level 1 is subtle, level 5 is dramatic.
coding.examples: .elevation-1 through .elevation-5 with increasing shadow depth
coding.hints: Negative spread values (-3px) make shadow start inside element edge. Two shadows: diffuse + sharp.
coding.solutionExplanation: Layered box-shadows with different offsets and blurs create realistic depth — mimicking physical object shadows.

coding.title: Padding vs Margin Decision Guide
coding.problemStatement: Write a function that given an element's role (button, section, list-item, card), returns the recommended spacing approach (margin vs padding) with reasoning.
coding.examples: button -> padding for click area, margin for spacing between buttons
coding.hints: Padding = space inside element. Margin = space outside between elements.
coding.solutionExplanation: Padding increases the element's background area and click target. Margin separates elements from their siblings.

coding.title: BFC Floats Clearfix
coding.problemStatement: Demonstrate the classic float layout problem and 3 solutions: (1) clearfix pseudo-element, (2) overflow:hidden BFC, (3) modern display:flow-root. Create CSS for all three.
coding.examples: Parent collapses when children are floated — all 3 fixes prevent this
coding.hints: All 3 create a BFC which forces the parent to contain its floated children.
coding.solutionExplanation: display: flow-root is the cleanest modern solution — it creates a BFC without the visual side effects of overflow: hidden.

coding.title: Positioning Cheatsheet
coding.problemStatement: Create a JS object documenting all 5 CSS position values with their key characteristics: static, relative, absolute, fixed, sticky.
coding.examples: {static: 'default, in normal flow', relative: 'offset from own position, stays in flow'}
coding.hints: static is the default. absolute and fixed are removed from flow. sticky is the modern scroll-aware positioning.
coding.solutionExplanation: Understanding when an element is in or out of flow is key: static and relative stay in flow; absolute and fixed do not.

coding.title: Tooltip Component
coding.problemStatement: Build a tooltip component: a button with a hidden tooltip that appears above on hover. Use position: absolute on the tooltip relative to a position: relative container. Include an arrow using a pseudo-element.
coding.examples: Tooltip appears above button, centered, with a downward arrow
coding.hints: bottom: calc(100% + 8px) positions above. translateX(-50%) centers it. ::after creates the triangle arrow.
coding.solutionExplanation: The CSS triangle trick: a block with zero width/height and only border on one side creates a triangle shape.

coding.title: Sticky Table Header
coding.problemStatement: Create a data table where the header row (thead) sticks to the top of the page as the user scrolls through a long table. Add a visual shadow to the sticky header.
coding.examples: position: sticky; top: 0 on th elements inside thead
coding.hints: sticky on th with top:0. The table wrapper needs overflow-y for the sticky to work within a scrollable container.
coding.solutionExplanation: sticky positioning works relative to the scrolling ancestor. The wrapper's overflow-y: auto makes it the scroll container.

coding.title: Stacking Context Visualizer
coding.problemStatement: Write a function that traverses the DOM and identifies all elements that create a new stacking context. Return their tag, class, and the property that triggered the stacking context.
coding.examples: [{el:'div.modal', trigger:'transform'}, {el:'header', trigger:'position+z-index'}]
coding.hints: getComputedStyle reads actual applied styles. Stacking context triggers: position+z-index, opacity<1, transform, filter, isolation.
coding.solutionExplanation: Stacking contexts are independent painting layers. z-index only competes within the same stacking context.

coding.title: Fixed Header with Scroll Offset
coding.problemStatement: Build a page with a fixed 64px header. Ensure anchor links (#section1) scroll to the correct position (not hidden under the header) using scroll-padding-top on the html element.
coding.examples: html { scroll-padding-top: 64px; } plus position:fixed header
coding.hints: scroll-padding-top offsets the scroll target position. padding-top on main prevents content hiding under fixed header.
coding.solutionExplanation: scroll-padding-top is the modern CSS solution to anchor link offset — no JavaScript needed.

coding.title: Dropdown Menu
coding.problemStatement: Create an accessible dropdown menu: a button that reveals a position:absolute menu on click. The menu should have z-index above other content, close on Escape key, and close when clicking outside.
coding.examples: Button triggers dropdown. position:absolute, z-index:50, top:100%
coding.hints: top: calc(100% + 4px) positions below the button. Click outside closes by checking closest('.dropdown').
coding.solutionExplanation: The outside-click handler uses event.target.closest('.dropdown') — if null, the click was outside all dropdowns.

coding.title: Image Overlay with Hover Effect
coding.problemStatement: Create an image card where on hover, a dark overlay fades in with centered text and a button. Use position:absolute for the overlay and transform for animation.
coding.examples: Overlay: position:absolute inset:0 opacity:0 hover:opacity:1
coding.hints: inset: 0 is shorthand for top:0 right:0 bottom:0 left:0. overflow:hidden clips the zoomed image.
coding.solutionExplanation: The zoom effect (scale) on the image combined with overflow:hidden on the container creates a contained zoom within the card bounds.

coding.title: Multi-level Dropdown
coding.problemStatement: Build a nested dropdown menu where hovering over a menu item reveals a sub-menu to the right. Use position:absolute on both menus and handle right-edge overflow by detecting available space.
coding.examples: Menu item hover -> sub-menu appears to the right at left:100%
coding.hints: left: 100% positions sub-menu to the right of parent. getBoundingClientRect detects if it overflows viewport.
coding.solutionExplanation: Checking getBoundingClientRect().right > window.innerWidth detects viewport overflow and flips the sub-menu direction.

coding.title: Parallax Scroll Effect
coding.problemStatement: Create a CSS parallax section: a background image that scrolls at a different speed than the foreground content. Use background-attachment: fixed and ensure it degrades for mobile (prefers-reduced-motion).
coding.examples: background-attachment: fixed creates parallax effect
coding.hints: background-attachment: fixed keeps background relative to viewport creating parallax. Disable for reduced motion users.
coding.solutionExplanation: CSS parallax with background-attachment:fixed is not supported on iOS (falls back to scroll). For cross-browser parallax, use transform: translateY in JS.

coding.title: Badge Component
coding.problemStatement: Create a notification badge that appears at the top-right corner of an icon button. Use position: absolute on the badge and position: relative on the container. Style it as a red circle with white count text.
coding.examples: Container is relative, badge is absolute top-right
coding.hints: Negative top and right values position badge overlapping the icon. border: 2px solid white creates separation.
coding.solutionExplanation: The white border around the badge visually separates it from the icon — a common pattern for notification indicators.

coding.title: Flexbox Navigation Bar
coding.problemStatement: Build a responsive navigation bar using Flexbox. Left side: logo. Right side: 3 nav links + a CTA button. On mobile (<768px), hide the nav links and show a hamburger button.
coding.examples: display:flex justify-content:space-between align-items:center
coding.hints: space-between pushes logo and links to opposite sides. gap adds space between nav items.
coding.solutionExplanation: Flexbox with justify-content:space-between is the standard approach for navbar layout — simple, no float hacks needed.

coding.title: Card Row Layout
coding.problemStatement: Create a row of 3 equal-width cards using Flexbox. Each card should have the same height (stretch to the tallest). Add a gap of 16px between cards.
coding.examples: flex: 1 on each card makes them equal width. align-items:stretch is default.
coding.hints: flex: 1 on each card = flex-grow:1 so they share available space equally. align-items:stretch is the default.
coding.solutionExplanation: Flexbox's default align-items:stretch makes all cards the same height as the tallest one — solving the equal-height card problem.

coding.title: Sticky Footer Layout
coding.problemStatement: Create a full-page layout where the footer always sticks to the bottom even when content is short. Use Flexbox on a wrapper with min-height:100vh and flex-direction:column.
coding.examples: wrapper: flex-direction:column min-height:100vh. main: flex:1
coding.hints: flex:1 on main-content makes it grow to fill available height, pushing footer down.
coding.solutionExplanation: flex:1 is the key: it makes main-content expand to fill all available space between header and footer.

coding.title: Holy Grail Layout
coding.problemStatement: Implement the classic Holy Grail layout: header, footer, and 3 columns (sidebar-left, main-content, sidebar-right) where main content is first in DOM for SEO. Use Flexbox.
coding.examples: Row: sidebar-left (200px) | main (flex:1) | sidebar-right (200px)
coding.hints: order property visually reorders without changing DOM order. Main comes first in DOM for SEO.
coding.solutionExplanation: The order property solves the DOM-vs-visual-order conflict: main first in DOM for SEO, visually in the center with order:2.

coding.title: Flex Wrapping Gallery
coding.problemStatement: Create a photo gallery using flex-wrap. Photos should be 200px wide, wrap onto multiple rows, and stretch to fill any leftover space on the last row using flex-grow.
coding.examples: flex-wrap:wrap. Each photo: flex: 0 1 200px (or 1 1 200px to grow)
coding.hints: flex: 1 1 200px means: can grow, can shrink, starts at 200px. This fills gaps on the last row.
coding.solutionExplanation: flex: 1 1 200px creates a responsive gallery where items are at least 200px wide and grow to fill leftover space on partial rows.

coding.title: Auto-fit Sidebar
coding.problemStatement: Build a layout that automatically switches between a sidebar+main layout (when viewport is wide) and a stacked layout (when narrow) using only Flexbox with flex-wrap. No media queries needed.
coding.examples: flex-wrap:wrap on container. Sidebar: flex: 0 0 240px. Main: flex: 1 1 400px (min content width)
coding.hints: When viewport is narrow, 240+400+24 > viewport width, so items wrap. No media query needed.
coding.solutionExplanation: When the sum of flex-basis values exceeds the container, flex-wrap moves items to new lines — creating responsive layout without media queries.

coding.title: Masonry-style Layout Approximation
coding.problemStatement: Approximate a masonry layout using Flexbox with flex-direction:column and flex-wrap:wrap. Set a fixed height on the container so items fill columns from top to bottom. Add a JS function to calculate the optimal container height.
coding.examples: flex-direction:column; flex-wrap:wrap; height:calculated
coding.hints: Column-direction flex-wrap needs explicit height to know when to start a new column.
coding.solutionExplanation: Column-direction flex-wrap fills columns top-to-bottom. The container height determines column breaks. This is an approximation — true masonry requires CSS Grid or JS.

coding.title: Responsive Form Layout
coding.problemStatement: Create a form where fields lay out in a 2-column grid on desktop using Flexbox. Fields with class .full-width span both columns. On mobile, all fields are single column.
coding.examples: flex-wrap:wrap. Each field: flex:1 1 calc(50% - 8px). .full-width: flex:0 0 100%
coding.hints: calc(50% - 8px) accounts for the gap between two columns. full-width uses flex-basis:100% to take full row.
coding.solutionExplanation: flex-basis: calc(50% - gap/2) creates two equal columns accounting for the gap. flex:0 0 100% forces a field to span the full width.

coding.title: Centered Hero Section
coding.problemStatement: Create a hero section that centers its content (heading, subtext, button) both vertically and horizontally using Flexbox. The section should take at least 100vh height.
coding.examples: display:flex; justify-content:center; align-items:center; flex-direction:column; min-height:100vh
coding.hints: flex-direction:column stacks items vertically. justify-content centers on main axis (vertical). align-items centers on cross axis (horizontal).
coding.solutionExplanation: For column flex containers, justify-content controls vertical alignment and align-items controls horizontal — opposite of row direction.

coding.title: Tab Component with Flex
coding.problemStatement: Build a tab bar where tabs are evenly distributed using Flexbox. The active tab has a bottom border indicator. Implement the JS to switch active tabs.
coding.examples: display:flex. Each tab: flex:1 for equal width. Active: border-bottom:2px solid blue
coding.hints: flex:1 makes all tabs equal width. border-bottom on .active creates the indicator. dataset.target links tab to panel.
coding.solutionExplanation: flex:1 on tabs creates equal-width distribution regardless of text length. The 2px bottom border indicator is a common pattern.

coding.title: Basic Grid Layout
coding.problemStatement: Create a 3-column grid layout for a blog: sidebar (250px), main content (flex:1), and ad column (200px). Stack vertically on mobile. Use CSS Grid with named areas.
coding.examples: grid-template-columns: 250px 1fr 200px. grid-template-areas defined
coding.hints: grid-template-areas creates a visual map of the layout. Grid area names must match grid-area properties on children.
coding.solutionExplanation: Named grid areas make the layout readable — the ASCII art of grid-template-areas documents the visual structure.

coding.title: Auto-fit Responsive Cards
coding.problemStatement: Create a card grid that automatically adjusts column count based on available space. Cards should be at minimum 280px wide, no media queries needed. Use repeat(auto-fit, minmax(280px, 1fr)).
coding.examples: repeat(auto-fit, minmax(280px, 1fr)) creates responsive columns automatically
coding.hints: auto-fit collapses empty tracks so items stretch to fill. minmax(280px, 1fr) sets minimum and maximum.
coding.solutionExplanation: repeat(auto-fit, minmax(280px, 1fr)) is the most powerful responsive layout technique — it adapts column count automatically without any media queries.

coding.title: Dashboard Layout
coding.problemStatement: Build a dashboard layout with: full-width top nav (60px), left sidebar (240px), right main area with 4 stat cards in a 2x2 grid, then a full-width chart area, then a table.
coding.examples: Grid: sidebar | [stats 2x2, chart, table]
coding.hints: Nested grids: outer grid for app layout, inner grid for content areas. grid-template-areas: 'nav nav' spans nav across both columns.
coding.solutionExplanation: Nested CSS Grid is ideal for complex layouts. The outer grid handles app structure; inner grids handle content sections independently.

coding.title: Magazine Layout
coding.problemStatement: Build a magazine-style layout where the first article is featured (spans 2 columns and 2 rows), and 4 smaller articles fill the remaining slots. Use grid-column and grid-row spanning.
coding.examples: Featured: grid-column:1/3; grid-row:1/3. Others: auto placement
coding.hints: grid-column: 1/3 spans from line 1 to line 3 (2 columns). grid-row: 1/3 spans 2 rows.
coding.solutionExplanation: Grid line numbers start at 1. grid-column: 1/3 means from grid line 1 to grid line 3 — spanning 2 columns.

coding.title: Calendar Grid
coding.problemStatement: Build a monthly calendar grid: 7 columns (Mon-Sun), dynamic rows for weeks. The first day may not start on Monday — use grid-column-start to offset the first day. Generate a full calendar for a given month.
coding.examples: If month starts on Wednesday, day 1 has grid-column-start:3
coding.hints: new Date(year, month, 1).getDay() returns 0 for Sunday. Empty divs offset the first day. Rows auto-create.
coding.solutionExplanation: CSS Grid automatically creates new rows when items overflow the 7 columns — no need to manually specify row count.

coding.title: Responsive Image Gallery with Grid
coding.problemStatement: Create a Pinterest-style layout using CSS Grid with grid-auto-rows. Tall images span multiple rows using JS to calculate and set grid-row-end based on content height.
coding.examples: grid-auto-rows:10px; item grid-row-end: span N where N = height/10
coding.hints: grid-auto-rows:10px creates tiny rows. Items span N rows using grid-row-end: span N. N = content height / row height.
coding.solutionExplanation: This technique creates true masonry: each item calculates how many 10px rows it needs to span its natural height.

coding.title: Pricing Table Grid
coding.problemStatement: Build a 3-column pricing table grid. The middle (Pro) plan should be highlighted and slightly taller, overlapping the others. Use z-index and transform: scale.
coding.examples: Middle card: transform:scale(1.05); z-index:1 to appear above neighbors
coding.hints: align-items:center on grid centers cards vertically. scale(1.05) makes featured card 5% larger. z-index prevents it from being covered.
coding.solutionExplanation: scale() on the featured card creates visual prominence without changing the layout flow — other cards don't shift.

coding.title: CSS Grid vs Flexbox Decision
coding.problemStatement: Write a function that recommends Grid or Flexbox given a layout description. Grid for 2D layouts; Flexbox for 1D (rows or columns). Return recommendation with reasoning.
coding.examples: 'card grid with varying heights' -> Grid. 'navbar' -> Flexbox
coding.hints: Grid: 2D control, both axes at once. Flexbox: 1D, one axis at a time. Often you use both together.
coding.solutionExplanation: Rule of thumb: if you need rows AND columns simultaneously use Grid. If items flow in one direction use Flexbox.

coding.title: Subgrid Alignment
coding.problemStatement: Use CSS Subgrid to align card contents (image, title, description, button) across multiple cards in a grid row. Each element in each card should be vertically aligned with its counterpart in adjacent cards.
coding.examples: grid-template-rows: subgrid on cards makes all rows align across cards
coding.hints: grid-row: span 4 on each card makes it span 4 rows. grid-template-rows: subgrid inherits parent row definitions.
coding.solutionExplanation: CSS Subgrid (grid-template-rows: subgrid) lets children participate in the parent grid's rows, enabling cross-card alignment.
